articles.title: Big Countries Meet to Talk About Oil Prices
articles.summary: The G7, a group of seven big countries, is having an emergency meeting on Monday because oil prices are going up fast. Oil is now over $100 a barrel, and stock markets are falling. This is happening because there is a war between the US, Israel, and Iran. The war is causing problems in the Strait of Hormuz, a key place where oil ships pass through. About one-fifth of the world's oil usually goes through there, but now it has almost stopped. This makes oil prices rise, which can make things more expensive for people and businesses. The G7 might release oil from their reserves to help lower prices. Over the weekend, there were more attacks in Iran and nearby countries, which scared the markets. Oil prices jumped by 25% in Asia on Monday before going down a bit.

articles.title: Ukraine Helps Middle East with Drone Defense
articles.summary: Ukraine is sending drone experts to the Middle East to help countries there. These countries are being attacked by drones from Iran. Ukraine has learned how to defend against drones because Russia has been using them against Ukraine for four years. Now, other countries want Ukraine's help to protect their skies. The BBC's reporter in Ukraine saw new anti-drone technology. The article also talks about other events in the Middle East, like attacks near a US building and criticism between leaders. It shows that the conflict is getting bigger in the region.

articles.title: Iran's Women's Football Team Needs Safety Help After Asian Cup
articles.summary: In Australia, people are worried about the safety of Iran's women's football team after they lost in the Asian Cup. The team, called the Iranian Lionesses, did not sing their national anthem in their first game, which made some people in Iran angry. Supporters in Australia cheered for the team and booed during the anthem because they do not like Iran's current government. They waved an old flag from before Iran's revolution. A former Australian football captain, Craig Foster, says the team should be kept safe because they played in a tournament. The team sang the anthem in later games, and some think they were forced to do it. Fans showed support, but the players could not talk much because they might be in danger when they go back to Iran.

articles.title: Oil Depots Hit in Iran: People Share Their Stories
articles.summary: In Iran, some oil depots were hit by strikes overnight, making the night sky very bright like daytime. People in cities like Tehran and Karaj saw big fires and explosions. They felt scared and confused because they didn't know what was happening. One person said they saw a red light and then a big cloud. Another said the smoke covered the city and they couldn't see the sun. The strikes are part of attacks by the US and Israel on Iran, which started after Iran's leader was killed. Iran has also attacked back. Authorities are checking the air quality and telling people to stay home for safety. The BBC talked to Iranians but kept their names secret to protect them.

articles.title: Colombia Votes for New Leaders and Congress
articles.summary: In Colombia, people voted on Sunday to choose a new Congress and pick candidates for the presidential election in May. The election happened with high security because of concerns about violence in some rural areas. There was an incident where many people tried to cross the border from Venezuela illegally to vote, which officials called fraud. The president and defense minister said they stopped buses and are investigating. The president also questioned the election software, but the registrar said it is safe and checked by international observers. Over 3,000 candidates ran for 285 seats in Congress, and more than 41 million people could vote. This election helps decide who will lead Colombia next.

articles.title: A Loud Bang at the U.S. Embassy in Norway
articles.summary: Early one Sunday morning, there was a loud bang outside the U.S. Embassy in Oslo, Norway. Police are investigating this explosion. No one was hurt, but the embassy's entrance was damaged. Officials think it was caused by a device in a backpack and that the embassy was the target. They are looking for the people who did it and why. Police are talking to witnesses and working hard to solve the case. The U.S. government is also checking what happened. Norway's leaders say this is a serious event, but there is no danger to the public.
